document:
Linenoise provides three user-facing features that make a prompt feel more like an interactive shell: history, completion, and hints. These features are optional and are controlled by small APIs in `linenoise.h`, so applications can choose only the parts they need.

History lets a program remember previous submitted lines and navigate them during editing. The public API includes `linenoiseHistoryAdd`, `linenoiseHistorySetMaxLen`, `linenoiseHistorySave`, and `linenoiseHistoryLoad`. The example program loads history from `history.txt` at startup, adds each non-empty non-command line after it is entered, and saves the history file back to disk. Inside the editor, Ctrl-P and the Up arrow move to older history entries, while Ctrl-N and the Down arrow move toward newer entries. The current in-progress line is temporarily stored into the history array before moving, so navigation can replace the edit buffer with a selected history entry.

Completion is callback-based. An application registers a completion callback with `linenoiseSetCompletionCallback`. When the user presses Tab and a callback is installed, linenoise calls the callback with the current buffer and a `linenoiseCompletions` object. The callback adds strings using `linenoiseAddCompletion`. In the example program, if the current buffer begins with `h`, the callback adds `hello` and `hello there` as possible completions. The completion loop cycles through candidates when Tab is pressed repeatedly, refreshes the edited line to display the candidate, and beeps when there are no completions or when the cycle reaches the original buffer state.

Completion state is tracked inside `struct linenoiseState` using fields such as `in_completion` and `completion_idx`. When completion mode is active, input is handled specially by `completeLine`. Escape cancels completion and restores the original buffer display. A normal typed character can accept the current completion by copying it into the edit buffer, then the returned character is processed as regular input. This design allows completion to behave interactively without replacing the entire edit loop.

Hints are also callback-based. An application registers a hints callback with `linenoiseSetHintsCallback`, and can optionally register a cleanup callback with `linenoiseSetFreeHintsCallback`. A hints callback receives the current buffer and can return a string to show to the right of the prompt, along with color and bold settings. The example program returns ` World` as a hint when the buffer equals `hello`, using color 35 and non-bold style. Hints are rendered as visual guidance; they are not inserted into the actual buffer unless the user types or completes text separately.

Together, history, completion, and hints provide higher-level interaction features on top of the raw editing buffer. History remembers previous accepted lines, completion proposes replacements for the current buffer, and hints display contextual help without changing the typed input.
